responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Techniques of Working With Resistance

D. S. Milman and G. D. Goldman. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1987, 417 pp., $37.50.

Jerome Siller PHD

Jerome Siller

Jerome Siller  

Milman and Goldman provide an excellent selection of writings ranging from the early seminal chapters to modern views. Freud's early formulation of resistance as being all those forces within the patient that oppose the treatment process was quite clear and direct. The writings sampled in this volume suggest that although we have not really gone beyond the essence of that statement, the elaborations and detailing add significantly to the clinical theory and practice of psychoanalysis.

The editors indicate that inherent in Freud's definition is the idea that resistances have both an intrapersonal and an interpersonal component, something that is apparent in the various chapters. The individual offering each add to the matrix of understanding of resistance phenomena. The specific focus of the contributor is quite predictable in the light of their generally extensive publication history. However, each proceeds to show how this fundamental clinical phenomenon can be perceived from his or her conceptual stance. That they succeed as well as they do speaks favorably to the vigor of the particular orientation being espoused. This serves as a reminder of how there may be a number of explanations for any phenomenon, the value of which must be assessed both within its own framework and in relation to other frameworks. In no instance are other frameworks considered, however, and the chapters are left to stand on their own.

“Basic Concepts of Resistance” is the first of three major divisions. An introductory chapter by the editors summarizes briefly the chapters to follow and proposes specific clinical applications from each. This is followed by classical papers on resistance by S. Freud, A. Freud, and Reich. Reich's work is a 74-page except from “Character Resistances.” Although some of the problems inherent in Reich's approach are mentioned in another selection, it is regrettable that Sterba's (1953) critique of Reich was not included in the book.

Nonsexist Language Requirements for APA Manuscripts

Carol Butler PHD

Carol Butler

Carol Butler  

In 1977, the American Psychological Association (APA) developed and published a set of guidelines for nonsexist language. In 1982, the Publications and Communications Board decided to require manuscripts submitted to any APA journal to use nonsexist language. I was dismayed recently to find sexist language (and awkwardly phrased nonsexist language) in this journal. When I investigated, I became aware that many of our members were not aware of the APA guidelines and requirement.

The APA guidelines are comprehensive and quite literate. They can be found in the Publications Manual, 3rd ed. The Manual is available for $12.50 for APA members and affiliates, plus $2.00 for shipping and handling. It may be ordered from the following address: APA Order Department, P.O. Box 2710, Hayattsville, MD 20784.

[[RunningHead]]

Development and Structure of the Body Image, Volumes 1 and 2

Seymour Fisher. Hillsdale, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum Associates, Inc., 1986, 871 pp., $39.95 and $60.00.

Franklin Shontz

Franklin Shontz

Franklin Shontz  

The term body image has appealed to clinicians, theorists, and the general public for many years, particularly since Paul Schilder (1950) brought psychoanalytic thought to bear on the topic. Most early thinking about the body image, including Schilder's, was based on evidence derived solely from clinical experience and informal observation. However, the methodology of mid-century American psychology was dominated by the epistemology of logical positivism. Within that philosophical framework, early theories about the body image were vulnerable to criticism for their lack of support from—or sometimes even for their advocates’ reluctance to appeal to—empirical validation. These theories seemed unduly speculative to investigators who were committed to quantification and traditional experimental research designs.

Once the interests of such investigators became aroused, however, their eagerness to study the body image empirically created a rapid proliferation of research on all aspects of body experience. Many early investigators were stimulated by Karen Machover's (1949) classic proposal that personality is projected into an individual's drawings of the human figure. Establishing the validity of these drawings as revelations of body images was difficult because so many other factors, such as artistic skill or training, enter into their production. But it was also difficult because no one knew exactly what a body image is.

As with the concept of intelligence, everyone seems to agree that such a thing as the body image exists, but no one is able to define the term satisfactorily. Early tests of intelligence could be developed because a specific indicator, success in school, was identified. Most people were willing to accept academic performance as a criterion for evaluating intelligence tests.

responseSet.authorCitation: Gedo, P. M. & Schaffer, N. D.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Empirical Approach to Studying Psychoanalytic Process

Paul Gedo PHD and Norman Schaffer PHD

Norman Schaffer

Norman Schaffer  

This study was a pilot attempt to investigate psychoanalytic process from an empirical perspective. We randomly selected early and late sessions from an audiotaped psychoanalysis (N = 324 sessions) and scored them using a modified form of the Gill-Hoffman (1982) system. We analyzed these scores via Markov models and comparative probability tests. The coding scheme was reliable, although the percentage of interrater agreement for ratings of patient insight was low. Despite this measurement error, the study yielded interesting findings concerning early and late hours. Across early and late sessions, interpretations facilitated patient transference insight, compared to other interventions within the same hours. The patient was somewhat more likely to follow one insight with a second during late hours. There were several other suggestions of longitudinal change toward greater patient mastery. We also explored the interrelationship between type of interpretation and patient productivity of transference insight. The findings indicated that the piloted method represents a useful way to pinpoint theoretically important interactions for empirical investigation.

responseSet.title: Intrapsychic and Behavioral Correlates of the Phenomenon of Imaginary Companions in Young Children
responseSet.authorMast: Jodie R. Meyer and  Steven Tuber
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intrapsychic and Behavioral Correlates of the Phenomenon of Imaginary Companions in Young Children

Jodie Meyer PHD and Steven Tuber PHD

Steven Tuber

Steven Tuber  

This study employed behavioral and Rorschach Test data to examine eighteen 4- and 5-year-olds with imaginary companions. We hypothesize that the imaginary companions served these children as an adaptive resource in handling conflictual interpersonal experience, thereby enabling them to function without overt symptomatology. As measured by the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) all the children did perform within the normal range. Their Rorschach protocols, when compared to normal, comparably aged children as reported by Ames, Metraux, Rodell, and Walker (1974), were characterized by a markedly larger number of human, animal, and inanimate movement scores and poorer form level. This Rorschach pattern suggested excellent imaginal resourcefulness and thus provided another demonstration of these children's remarkable capacity for symbolic representation. An analysis of the content of these responses using the Mutuality of Autonomy (MOA) Scale (Urist, 1977) revealed a contrast between autonomous, benign human interactive percepts and malevolent animal and inanimate movement interaction. We viewed the use of the human movement response as a buffer against more conflictual fantasied representations. This perspective is parallel to previous clinical studies that stressed the reparative function the imaginary companion serves in handling conflict.

responseSet.title: Object Constancy: The Illusion of Being Seen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Constancy

The Illusion of Being Seen

Avance Sherwood PHD

Avance Sherwood

Avance Sherwood  

Recent theoretical advances in the concept of object constancy have placed it in the context of the child's establishing a separate identity. Although these advances were partly the result of examining the child's growing abilities to evoke a mental image of the absent mother, constancy cannot be well understood as a type of mental representation. It more resembles an illusion, specifically the illusion that the mother is constantly available in her mirroring function. The child's sense of his or her own reality is born in the mother's affectively attuned mirroring, as nothing about the child is real for the child until first seen by the mother. The illusion of the constant object allows the child to construct a sense of his or her own separate reality.

responseSet.title: Mental Imagery in Psychoanalytic Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mental Imagery in Psychoanalytic Treatment

John Suler

John Suler

John Suler  

Mental imagery techniques may facilitate the therapeutic process by stimulating patients' insight into unconscious dynamics by helping them uncover and master warded-off affect, and by enhancing the clinician's empathic contact and access to countertransference. The history of imagery techniques in the psychoanalytic movement and the effect of these techniques on the traditional psychoanalytic method are reviewed. A conceptual framework based on the theory of primary and secondary process suggests spontaneity, experiential scope, associative elaboration, and object impact as four dimensions for the clinical evaluation of imagery experience.

responseSet.title: Premises of Interpersonal Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Premises of Interpersonal Theory

Herbert Zucker PHD

Herbert Zucker

Herbert Zucker  

Premises of interpersonal theory are elucidated in order to crystallize its foundations and to point up the utility of discussing psychoanalytic theory on the level of premises. In regard to human psychology, interpersonal theory assumes an inextricable interpenetration of biology and external influences. It therefore questions assigning biology or “biologized” concepts predominance in explaining meaning or the contents of the mind. Biology is implicated in many interpersonal concepts, but not in the form implied in such concepts as drive or inherently arising endogenous states. Rather than an unfolding general nature, interpersonal theory assumes an outcome emerging from the interaction of manifold biological potentials with complex social influences. Such a perspective implies further assumptions about human impressibility, social determinism, psycho/social multidimensionality, complexity, and uniqueness. These assumptions lead to a focus on individual psychology, on character, for example, rather than general psychology, and on holistic/field conceptions of causality rather than the causal linkage of relatively isolated dimensions. Emphasis on drive and endogenous states eventuates in an underestimation of the complexity of personality formation and of the subtlety and power of the psycho/social experiential interaction.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial Note

Bertram Cohler PHD

Bertram Cohler

Bertram Cohler  

With this issue, Psychoanalytic Psychology introduces a new section entitled Commentary. This section is reserved for reader response to articles published in the Journal. The Editorial Board believes that this section might enhance communication among colleagues differing in their views regarding theory and clinical phenomena in psychoanalysis and that it might facilitate increased appreciation of diverse approaches. Manuscripts are invited which address theoretical or clinical issues posed by previously published articles. Ordinarily, such responses are expected to be 10 pages or less, typed, and double-spaced in APA format. Although the Editor may consult with members of the Editorial Board, every effort will be made to expedite acceptance and publication of commentary. On occasion, the Editor will also solicit commentary from among reviewers of accepted manuscripts and colleagues working in the area addressed by an article.

* * * * *

The Editorial Board is considering a proposal for an occasional section of the Journal devoted to reports of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Although the primary mission of Psychoanalytic Psychology concerns theoretical and clinical perspectives in psychoanalysis, we recognize that there is limited opportunity for publication of articles reporting on the application of psychoanalytic concepts to individual, family, and group psychotherapy, as well as to the supervision of psychotherapy, and that the Journal might facilitate such communication. Review of these articles is particularly difficult: Innovations in technique or observations on clinical process which the author finds helpful are difficult for reviewers to evaluate. The Editorial Board and Publications Committee of Division 39 invites reader response to this proposal, including suggestions for criteria to be used in reviewing these articles. The address for the Editorial Office is shown on the inside Journal cover.

The Dance of Anger: A Woman's Guide to Changing Patterns of Intimate Relations

H. G. Lerner. New York: Harper & Row, 1985, 239 pp., $8.95 (paperback)

Harold Fine PHD

Harold Fine

Harold Fine  

When James McKeen Cattell arrived at Wundt's laboratory in Leipzig, Germany, in the early 1880s, he brought with him a new machine—a typewriter. Wundt was amazed by it and uttered the historical words, “Ganze Amerikanisch” (totally American). Alongside the American tradition of invention, aimed at improving the quality of life, is the tradition of mind curing of self-improvement, which stems from and is manifested in such people and movements as Ralph Waldo Trine, Mary Baker Eddy, Emerson's transcendentalism, Dale Carnegie, Norman Vincent Peale, Southern California's human potential movement, Est, and the New Age Revivalism, with its trend of narcissism so amply documented in the 1970s and 1980s. This tradition has gradually become institutionalized in pop psychology and even in health-care provisions as we move into a postindustrial age. In fact, I recently received an inquiry about establishing a franchise for an enuresis center that employed behavior-modification techniques. A week does not go by in my practice that some circuit rider does not contact me about workshops on Neuro Linguistic Programming (NLP), marketing strategies, women in treatment, the borderline. American Psychological Association (APA) members are suing to become psychoanalysts, to get into Health Maintenance Organizations (HMOs) and Preferred Provider Organizations (PPOs), and to join a burgeoning video industry. Whatever happened to our intellectual and scholarly roots? As every endeavor becomes business, young colleagues, whom I supervise, no longer talk about their practices but about their businesses.

The Menninger Foundation—established in the 1920s in Topeka, KS, as part of Middle America's reaction to the too-European, too-Jewish, and too-sexual Freudian psychoanalysis—has served as an innovator in the pragmatics of treatment and training. responseSet.title: Free Association and the Division of Labor in Psychoanalytic Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Free Association and the Division of Labor in Psychoanalytic Treatment

Peter Newton PHD

Peter Newton

Peter Newton  

Although Freud considered the rule of free association to be fundamental, he was tentative about the recommendations he made concerning other aspects of analysis. Three quarters of a century later, there is still no formal theory of the working arrangements in treatment, and even the fundamental rule is considered by analytic clinicians to be optional. I portray therapy as a dyadic social system and examine its primary task, boundaries, divisions of labor and authority, and culture in order to weigh the importance of the fundamental rule to task achievement. I find several advantages to making free association a role requirement upon the patient, including, inter alia, the freedom it provides the therapist for relaxed observation, counterassociation, and thought.

responseSet.title: Vicissitudes in the Psychotherapy of Depressive Crises
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vicissitudes in the Psychotherapy of Depressive Crises

John Gedo MD

John Gedo

Chicago, IL

John Gedo  

Depressive crises may drive patients into psychotherapy, but they can also supervene in the course of treatment of patients with severe personality disorders. In such contingencies, pharmacotherapy may be helpful and should never be neglected; however, mastery of the affective crisis is only preparation for dealing psychotherapeutically with its cause. This dynamic usually involves collapse of illusions, either about the perfection of another or about the absence of personal limitations. Psychoanalysis proper is required to eliminate such vulnerabilities and to deal with the patient's defenses against archaic feeling states. The recurrence of these feeling states in the transference may, however, rupture the therapeutic bond.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Process: Theory, Clinical Observation and Empirical Research

Joseph Weiss and Harold Sampson and the Mount Zion Psychotherapy Research Group. New York: Guilford, 1986, 347 pp. + appendices, $49.95.

Stanley Messer PHD

Stanley Messer

Stanley Messer  

It is rare enough to come across a new psychoanalytically-based theory of psychopathology and psychotherapy. It is rarer still to encounter a program of empirical research on any psychoanalytic topic, let alone one that employs clinical case material from a bona fide psychoanalysis. Weiss and Sampson's project has accomplished this and more: The authors have tied their theory of psychopathology to notions of what fosters therapeutic progress and have then taken the unusual step of testing these ideas systematically and scientifically. Their book will appeal especially to those who are receptive to a cognitively oriented psychoanalytic approach and who advocate more informed, scientifically rigorous psychoanalytic research.

Weiss and Sampson's theory is based on the ego psychology of Freud and his followers, such as Kris, Hartmann, Rapaport, and Sandler. It is grounded particularly in what the authors call “the higher mental functioning” hypothesis (HMF), which postulates that people can exert some control over their unconscious mental life, including their repressed beliefs. The authors refer to Freud's later work, in which he “assigned a much greater part to those agencies of the mind that are acquired by learning from experience, that think and anticipate, make decisions and plans, and direct criticism against the self” (p. 31). This may be contrasted with Freud's “automatic functioning hypothesis” (AF) which proposed that unconscious mental life is regulated by the pleasure principle, which functions automatically without regard for thought, belief, or reality.

The Psychotic Core

Michael Eigen. New York: Aronson, 1986, xii + 387 pp., $30.00

Robert Rogers PHD

Robert Rogers

Robert Rogers  

In his Preface, Michael Eigen states that he intends to review various “basic ideas” pertaining to the dynamics of psychosis deriving from the work of Freud, Jung, recent object-relations theory, and self psychology; to critique these ideas; and to contribute “a working orientation that does madness justice” (vi). By doing madness justice, Eigen means doing justice to the pervasiveness of it, the trace of craziness even in everyday life. “The view that a basic madness marks human affairs cannot easily be avoided,” he claims (vi). As Meissner does in The Paranoid Process (1978), Eigen treats what might be called “psychotic orientations” as part of a spectrum of processes ranging from those of essentially normal people to the wildest, most self-destructive behavior of the certifiably insane. Correspondingly, Eigen does not see the analyst as occupying an antiseptic mental space carefully demarked from that of his patients. He rather insists, as Searles (1965) so notably does, on the partly participatory nature of the analyst' role.

What most distinguishes Eigen' volume from other approaches to the subject is the manner in which he insists on defining psychosis not nosologically, or taxonomically, according to more or less received notions about types and categories of pathology, but rather in terms of a particular set of “invariant” he claims characterizes psychotic processes in general. He chooses to concentrate on six of these “principal elements” of psychosis, admitting freely that these are conceptual elements he finds he cannot personally do without in the clinical encounter, and that other practitioners might well wish to consider other factors. There is nothing especially controversial about the principal elements he selects: hallucination, mindlessness, boundary demarcation (of self and other), hate, epistemology (reality testing, self-definition),

responseSet.title: On History and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On History and Psychoanalysis

Malcolm Pines FRCP, FRCPsych, DPM

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines  

Freud claimed that psychoanalysis represented a major assault on human narcissism. This view is only partially correct for it is largely ahistorical. Freud's view must now be balanced against the historians' perspective on psychoanalysis, which in its turn represents a potential narcissistic blow to psychoanalysis, so long as psychoanalysts isolate themselves from fuller recognition of the sociocultural matrix of Freud's work.

This article, by a psychoanalyst, presents some of the newer perspectives of historians on the development of Freud's work against the background of late 19th century Austrian and German political, cultural, and social history. Through understanding this past, we are better able to understand the present dilemmas of psychoanalysis, in particular the relevance of social forces in the development of emotional disorder.

responseSet.title: Rorschach Images of Symbiosis and Separation in Eating-Disordered and in Borderline and Nonborderline Subjects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rorschach Images of Symbiosis and Separation in Eating-Disordered and in Borderline and Nonborderline Subjects

Laurence Hirshberg PHD

Laurence Hirshberg

Laurence Hirshberg  

The Rorschach protocols of 62 eating-disordered women were analyzed for content expressing symbiotic, separation, and counter-symbiotic strivings. Subjects were grouped into three symptom pattern groups and into borderline and nonborderline groups as measured by Gunderson's Diagnostic Interview for Borderlines (DIB). Very few differences in this content were found among the three symptom-based groups. Consistent and significant differences were found among these groups in the relation between overall Rorschach response productivity and the production of responses with this content. This suggests that the symptom patterns may reflect differing modes of self-regulation. Consistent and significant differences in the production of this content were also found between borderline and nonborderline groups, supporting clinically based claims that symbiotic Rorschach responses are diagnostic of borderline functioning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Errors and Misconstruals in the Reporting of Freud's Work

An Illustration

Jay Land PHD

Jay Land

Jay Land  

In an article published in this journal, Gornick (1986) discussed the situation of the female analyst or therapist and the male patient. In attempting to establish a “new narrative,” she made reference to Freud's ideas about transference. However, many of the references misstate or misconstrue Freud's position. This thesis is illustrated by a detailed examination of five errors contained in a brief section of Gornick's article entitled “The Erotic Transference.” The article closes with a plea for scholarly accuracy in portraying the positions of those with whom one disagrees.

[[RunningHead]]

Resistance: Psychodynamic and Behavioral Approaches

Paul L. Wachtel. New York: Plenum, 1982, 267 pp.

Stuart Twemlow MD

Stuart Twemlow

Stuart Twemlow  

This three-part edited collection of chapters considers the protean problem of resistance in psychotherapy. The term is defined and considered from several radically different psychological perspectives; the psychoanalytic approach in Part I consists of four chapters written by authorities in the field. The four chapters of Part II concern both cognitive and pure behavioral approaches. Part III contains eight brief chapters in which the authors were invited to comment on one another's contributions.

In the main, the authors remain focused and concise, but it is very obvious that resistance has a remarkably broad variety of definitions, and that some of these definitions are so different that the authors do not appear to be speaking of similar phenomena.

What is also clear is that these phenomena are quite important to all psychotherapists, of almost any theoretical persuasion. The book is about not so much classical “transference resistance,” although it deals with this. On a more generalized level, it is about patients who do not change when they ought to, in spite of a “sane” therapist's best efforts. Wachtel tentatively defines resistance as:

… not something that periodically comes up to disrupt theory. It is the track of the patient's conflict about changing, the way in which the sincere desire to change confronts the fears, misconceptions, and prior adaptative strategies that make change difficult. (p. xix)

This definition reflects a remarkably narrow-minded focus on the patient as the “resistor.

responseSet.title: A New Society Formed to Promote Quantitative Psychoanalytic Research
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A New Society Formed to Promote Quantitative Psychoanalytic Research

Many have stated that the lack of quantitative data supporting psychoanalytic theory derived from controlled and replicable designs makes psychoanalysis vulnerable to intense criticism. This criticism comes from both within and without the psychoanalytic community.

Although the number of professional organizations within our psychoanalytic specialty has grown exponentially, until recently, there was no single organization specifically devoted to the promotion of quantitative research on matters significant to psychoanalytic theory and practice.

The Society for the Advancement of Quantitative Research in Psychoanalysis (SAQRP) was recently formed to fill this vacuum.

The Society is currently developing a membership directory. A 1-day conference and a research newsletter are planned for next year.

If interested, please mail your name, address, and telephone number to the Society' President, Dennis G. Shulman, PhD, SAQRP, Fordham University, The College at Lincoln Center, 113 West 60th Street, Room 916, New York, NY 10023. You may also include a statement summarizing any quantitative psychoanalytic research you have completed or any specific topic in which you have an interest.

[[RunningHead]]

Empirical Studies of Psychoanalytic Theories, Volume 2

Joseph M. Masling. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1986, 226 pp., $29.95.

Richard Billow PHD

Richard Billow

Richard Billow  

Masling's Empirical Studies of Psychoanalytic Theories, Volume 2, consists of seven chapters. With one exception, each author reviews his or her own programmatic research, along with related empirical and theoretical findings. This was also the format in Volume 1. I found a commonality in these contributions—representing as they do vital, but varied approaches to experimental psychoanalytic psychology—an interest in the question: What is the nature and the development of thinking and how does it vary in psychopathological states?

The early Freud established a portion of the conscious ego as the developing organ of thinking. But like the Devil in Milton, Freud's Unconscious was clearly the most interesting character even in a theory of thought, organizing experience from birth, but not in an orderly and morally proper manner. Unconscious processes pay “little regard to reality. They are subject to the pleasure principle; their fate depends only on how strong they are and on whether they fulfill the demands of the pleasure-unpleasure regulation.” (Freud, 1915, p. 187, as quoted by Weiss & Sampson, p. 4).

In Freud's later theorizing the unconscious, while still contrasted to the thinking ego, was attributed a wider variety of maturational and homeostatic mental functions. Further, rather than being solely id motivated, the unconscious was also motivated by forces emanating from the ego or superego.

[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations Family Therapy

David E. Scharff and Jill Savage Scharff. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1987, 503 pp., $40.00

Sherwin Radin MD

Sherwin Radin

Syracuse, NY

Sherwin Radin  

In Object Relations Family Therapy, David and Jill Scharff illustrate the application of the British object relations theory to psychoanalytic work with individuals, couples, and families. The basis for their approach rests primarily on Fairbairn's theoretical constructions, and—to a lesser extent—on some of the contributions of Winnicott, Guntrip, Balint, Klein, and Bion. The authors stress the utilitarian value of object relations theory to interpersonal interactions characteristic of families and couples. In contrast, classical psychoanalytic instinct theory is essentially an intrapsychic frame of reference, useful in individual psychoanalysis, but of limited empirical value in group work.

The Scharffs demonstrate how one can apply Fairbairnian principles, with some modifications, to understanding families from theoretical, developmental, and therapeutic perspectives. Fairbairn indicates that inevitable dissatisfaction with the maternal object is the only motive for object integration. The Scharffs state that not only is there defensive object introjection as Fairbairn describes, but that the internal object is also derived from pleasure inherent in object relations themselves.

Another illustration of Fairbairn's concepts applied to family relationships is the repression of the libidinal system by the antilibidinal ego and the rejecting object. The Scharffs see this mechanism operating when couples fight and complain but neither person is able to speak of his or her underlying hurt and longing. The authors add to Fairbairn's mechanisms the concept of the libidinal system repressing the antilibidinal system, for example, when couples repress their contempt by mutual expressions of affection and concern.

The Scharffs' technique of family therapy emphasizes developmental family interactions. They develop concepts of centered holding and contextual holding from early object relations. These holding concepts are vital components of the authors' object relations family therapy theory and technique.

responseSet.title: Linguistic Considerations in the Treatment of Bilinguals
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Linguistic Considerations in the Treatment of Bilinguals

Rafael Javier PHD

Rafael Javier

Rafael Javier  

Language is intimately connected with most aspects of human development. Because of this pervasive quality, language is incorporated as part of the individual's coping mechanisms, and at times it may even serve a definite defensive role. This investigation examines research and clinical data regarding the language independence phenomenon as it relates to the treatment of bilingual patients. Attempts are made to describe the way the bilingual individuals mobilize (shift) their languages under anxiety-producing conditions and how this shifting of languages can be utilized by these individuals as part of their coping mechanisms. There can be both positive and negative implications for treatment. In this regard, the article illustrates how the linguistic shifting may further reinforce defenses such as intellectualization, splitting, and isolation of affect. Linguistic shifting can also be technically introduced by monolingual clinicians to advance the therapeutic process.

[[RunningHead]]

Failed Empathy—A Central Theme in the Survivor's Holocaust Experience

Dori Laub MD and Nanette Auerhahn PHD

Nanette Auerhahn

Nanette Auerhahn  

In this article, we concern ourselves with massive traumatic events deliberately inflicted on individuals by fellow human beings. Specifically, we focus on the Holocaust and the fact that massive failure of the environment to mediate needs, as in genocide, will throw into question the existence of empathy, human communication, and ultimately one's own humanity, to which any mirroring ceases to exist. Such a life experience will represent, to the survivor of trauma, failure of a responsive empathic agent or function. Because it is precisely representations of need-satisfying interactions that provide the basis for links between personal existence and social connectedness, undermining the individual's representation of the need-mediating context will deconstruct the link between self and other. Destruction of the victim's representational matrix of interpersonal relatedness results in a vulnerability and loneliness in his or her internal world representation which is the sine qua non of man-made trauma.

Trust in the world includes … the certainty that the other person will spare me … that he will respect my physical and metaphysical being. responseSet.title: A Revised Psychoanalytic Conception of Schizophrenia: An Interdisciplinary Update
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Revised Psychoanalytic Conception of Schizophrenia

An Interdisciplinary Update

James Grotstein MD

James Grotstein

James Grotstein  

The psychoanalytic conception and treatment of schizophrenia is in need of fundamental revision because of (a) refinements in nosology which have separated out the borderline and affective disorders with which it had formerly been confused; (b) the development of newer psychoanalytic schools; and (c) the impact of various aspects of neurobiological research, the putative outcome of which seems to confirm that many, if not most, schizophrenics begin with a hereditary and/or congenital vulnerability to life and are disadvantaged in how they experience life, not only emotionally, but also neuroperceptually, neurocognitively, and neurobehaviorally. This contribution seeks to put schizophrenia and psychoanalysis in an interdisciplinary perspective by assigning the concept of disorders of psychical meaningfulness to the neurotic portion of the schizophrenic personality and of psychical meaninglessness to the psychotic portion of the personality. Whereas all psychoanalytic schools traditionally employ models that address meaningfulness, I suggest the employment of a newer model to deal with schizophrenia as a “disorder of self-regulation” (of meaninglessness), the latter being the neurobiological contribution. To the former I should like also to append the concept of a “disorder of interactional regulation” as its interpersonal complement in the sense of psychosocial, object relations, and systems theory interactions.

responseSet.authorMast: Fred Wright,  John O'Leary and  Joseph Balkin
responseSet.authorCitation: Wright, F., O'Leary, J. & Balkin, J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shame, Guilt, Narcissism, and Depression

Correlates and Sex Differences

Fred Wright PHD , John O'Leary PHD and Joseph Balkin PHD

Joseph Balkin

Joseph Balkin  

This study examines the relationships between shame, guilt, narcissism, and depression and the gender differences connected with these states. We hypothesize that shame would be a more powerful contributor to narcissism and depression than guilt and that women would score higher in shame, higher in depression, and lower in narcissism than men. The Adapted Shame/Guilt Scale, the Narcissistic Personality Inventory, and the Self-Rating Depression Scale were administered to male and female undergraduates.

The results show that shame plays an important role in both narcissism and depression, and that its role in depression is stronger than that of guilt. It was also concluded that men are more prone to narcissism and women to depression, and sex differences related to shame and guilt are linked to specific situations and self-images. Implications for treatment are also noted.

responseSet.title: Projective Identification: Whose Projection—Whose Identity?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Projective Identification

Whose Projection—Whose Identity?

Harriet Knapp PHD

Harriet Knapp

Harriet Knapp  

An examination of the concept of projective identification indicates that it is incongruent with a definition as a primitive defense mechanism. Its application to countertransference as currently in the literature has been misleading. It is redefined here as a transactional process, where the projection contains a quality of intensity or power leading to induction of aspects of the projection, but viewing the induction as a function of the recipient's vulnerability due to identity fragmentation. Implications for transference/countertransference issues in the analytic milieu are discussed.

responseSet.title: Oneness Fantasies Revisited
responseSet.authorMast: Frank M. Lachmann and  Beatrice Beebe
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Oneness Fantasies Revisited

Frank Lachmann PHD and Beatrice Beebe PHD

Beatrice Beebe

Beatrice Beebe  

The research program initiated by Lloyd Silverman on effects of the subliminal activation of oneness fantasies concluded that unconscious oneness fantasies can enhance adaptation if, simultaneously, a sense of self can be preserved. However, formulations with respect to the developmental origins of these fantasies and their ubiquitous role in pathology and everyday life may have overemphasized the contribution of the fantasy of a oneness experience and under-estimated the equally important contribution of the experience of a preserved, intact sense of self. To redress this imbalance, oneness experiences and sense of self are proposed to define the endpoints of a dimension of experience optimally characterized by a flexibility to span this range. Although we assume that experiences throughout the life span contribute to this dimension, we use the data from empirical infant research to suggest that early experiences of matching, disruptions, and repair of ruptures in mother-infant interactive attunements make a relevant contribution to its establishment. It is suggested that this range of experience is represented and subjected to symbolic elaborations, a process that continues throughout life. The oneness fantasy is proposed as a product of symbolic elaboration, a fantasy formation of later childhood or the adult mind.

[[RunningHead]]

The I

Norman N. Holland. New Haven and London: Yale University Press, 1985, 390 pp., $30.00.

Robert Seidenberg MD

Robert Seidenberg

Syracuse, NY

Robert Seidenberg  

If it ever came to a point that psychoanalysis had to be entrusted to anyone, my vote would go to a professor of English. By training and experience, such a person knows about metaphors and their meaning in the human experience. An English professor would never be found examining the ear of a person who heard God's voice. Similarly, an English professor, even one who was also an electrical engineer (Professor Holland), would not apply amperometrics to an organ or limb that had been “cathected,” or recommend vasoligation for preserving the psychic energies of schizophrenics. The literary person becomes puzzled when clinicians find specific chemicals to energize the ego or reinforce the superego. Psychoanalysis and the literary experience both start and end with words and abstractions, all fed by the symbolic process. It is, therefore, neither surprising nor accidental that a professor of English (and a psychoanalyst), Norman N. Holland, should bring us illumination in the direction of humanity in an age when the mechanistic theme has become so prevalent in psychology, psychiatry, and sadly, in psychoanalysis as well. Holland informs us of individuality, of uniqueness apart from the statistical heavy hand. Acknowledging his debt to Erikson and Lichtenstein, Holland uses the concept of identity to bring into coherence an individual's lifelong behavior. He views identity—as a literary person would—as a theme or style, albeit with variations, that characterizes an individual from inchoation to the very end. Obstructions and interferences with the identity theme constitute frustrations, which in turn lead to crises and to the bevy of aberrations observed in the consultation room, in life, and in literature. In other words, we track the exquisitely fragile “I” and its vicissitudes. Although we are constantly doing new things, our “style” never changes. For Holland then,

responseSet.title: Diagnosis and Distancing Reactions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Diagnosis and Distancing Reactions

Bonnie Wolfe PHD

Bonnie Wolfe

Los Angeles

Bonnie Wolfe  

The possibility that diagnostic formulations can impede rather than promote the goals of psychotherapy is discussed in the context of the intersubjective nature of the therapeutic relationship. Psychoanalytic literature regarding diagnosis and analyzability is reviewed to show how assumptions about the permanence of pathological structures or defects in patients' personalities have shaped and limited clinicians' sensibilities. A clinical illustration shows how attitudes implicit in diagnostic formulations can inhibit the development of a transference bond and the resumption of a thwarted developmental process. Concluding comments relate distancing reactions to empathic limitations of the therapist, which may be reflected in diagnoses, in decisions regarding technique, and in conceptualizations of transference and treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Between the “Science” and the “Art” of Interpretation

Freud' Method of Interpreting Dreams

Mark Freeman PHD

Mark Freeman

Mark Freeman  

Despite the value of recent attempts to consider the process of psychoanalytic interpretation less in scientific terms than in artistic terms, the end result is both the perpetuation of a seemingly unbridgeable gap between “science” and “hermeneutics” and the failure to develop an adequate conceptualization of the process of interpretation itself. It is argued in this article that Freud' method of interpretation, specifically his method of interpreting dreams, may serve as a useful vehicle for allowing us to think beyond the aforementioned gap and for more adequately conceptualizing the process of interpretation in general.

responseSet.title: Stagnation, Chaos, and Severe Character Neuroses
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stagnation, Chaos, and Severe Character Neuroses

Michael Diamond PHD

Michael Diamond

Los Angeles

Michael Diamond  

There are a relatively large number of unexplored issues in treating severely neurotic, character-disordered patients. The defining characteristics of these patients are formulated in dynamic, structural, and object relational terms. A discussion of the masochistic character is presented to exemplify such features. It is suggested that treatment is an inescapable struggle due to the distinctly created transference-countertransference ambience wherein character resistances emerge as therapeutic stalemates. The milieus characterized by stagnation and chaos are examined in relation to clinical examples. Finally, three essential technical issues are considered in terms of therapists’ capacity to “hold” patients’ externalized material, use the countertransference, and sooner or later, interpret primitive defensive constellations and transferences.

responseSet.title: Self Psychology and the Analysis of the Superego
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self Psychology and the Analysis of the Superego

Lawrence Josephs PHD

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs  

The thesis of this article is that self psychology can be construed as a superego psychology in that both involve an intensive study of the self-regulatory and self-evaluative functions of the mind and their precursors in early object relations. Kohut's clinical contribution can be redefined in terms of the structural model as the delineation of the regressive, pathological, and healthy methods through which the ego attempts to restore the approval of the superego. Kohut's grandiose self can be seen as a precursor of the ego ideal and his idealized parent imago as a precursor of conscience. Conflict between the ego ideal and the conscience constitutes a vital aspect of intrapsychic functioning which self psychology has neglected in its focus upon developmental arrest. Sexual and aggressive aims play an important role in regaining the approval of the superego through their unique capacity to evoke a sense of the omnipotence of the corporeal self.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Formation of Delusions

Bertram Karon PHD

Bertram Karon

Bertram Karon  

Four principles are identified that account for most delusion formation. The most important of these is transference—transference to the world at large. The second is the defense against pseudohomosexual impulses as described by Freud (and generally misunderstood). The third is the learning within the family of bizarre meanings of concepts which are assumed erroneously to be the meanings other people use. The fourth is the need to have a more or less consistent understanding of one's life and experiences. Therapeutic implications are drawn.

responseSet.title: Psychodynamic Play Therapy With a High-Functioning Autistic Child
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Play Therapy With a High-Functioning Autistic Child

Richard Bromfield PHD

Richard Bromfield

Richard Bromfield  

The increasingly dominant and sound view of autism as a developmental disorder has led to adoption of highly structured behavioral interventions and abandonment of psychoanalytic approaches. This article intends to reaffirm the relevance of traditional psychodynamic play therapy to the milder variants of the autistic syndrome. The therapy process with one high-functioning autistic child is examined and discussed in the context of autistic pathology and psychodynamic theory.

responseSet.title: Traumatic Moment, Basic Dangers and Annihilation Anxiety
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Traumatic Moment, Basic Dangers and Annihilation Anxiety

Marvin Hurvich PHD

Marvin Hurvich

Marvin Hurvich  

It is contended that the fear of being overwhelmed or annihilated is a correlate of Freud' traumatic moment, constitutes a basic danger, and should be included in the series of fear of loss of the object, loss of love, castration, and superego censure. Traumatic and signal anxiety are conceptualized as two points in a series of potential responses, and it is assumed that mental representations of the early state of helplessness (originally preverbal) can later be anticipated. Major implications of annihilation anxiety noted by Freud and later psychoanalysts are reviewed, and are found to reflect fears of disintegration of the ego and of the self. Though it has been cited many times, the concept is relatively undeveloped. Key factors which increase the likelihood of annihilation anxiety are identified as traumatic experiences, ego weaknesses, and threats to self-cohesion. The prevalence of annihilation anxieties at various levels of psychopathology, as compared with other anxiety contents, is reviewed.

responseSet.title: Gender and Transference: Conversations With Female Analysts
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender and Transference

Conversations With Female Analysts

Nancy Kulish PHD

Nancy Kulish

Southfield, MI

Nancy Kulish  

The possible influence of analysts’ gender on the transference has received attention in recent psychoanalytic writing. Interviews with 17 senior female analysts about their views on this question are summarized. Most felt that analysts’ gender does affect the transference, especially in the sequence of material's emergence, and should be taken into consideration in referrals. Possible influences on the “maternal” and “paternal” transferences, the erotic and “eroticized” transferences, and the oedipal transference were also discussed. Gender-related biases or countertransferences were suggested.

responseSet.title: Incest and the Borderline Syndrome: The Mediating Role of Identity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Incest and the Borderline Syndrome

The Mediating Role of Identity

Barbara Marcus PHD

Barbara Marcus

Barbara Marcus  

This article reviews psychoanalytic theory, clinical studies, and empirical investigations regarding the pathological effects of father-daughter incest. This literature suggests strong links between childhood incest and serious psychopathological sequelae, particularly borderline personality disorder. I propose a model for understanding the impact of incest, using the concept of identity as a mediating construct. Particular emphasis is placed on the role played by incest in the disruption of the developing boundary between internal and external reality. Such defects may result in the failure of age-appropriate divestment of the illusion of omnipotent control and the inappropriate persistence of efforts to control others. Thus, such disruptions may account for major aspects of borderline syndrome including identity diffusion, affective instability, and primitive defenses. A case vignette is presented as an illustration of the model's cogency with respect to the treatment of a borderline incest victim. Theoretical, treatment, and research implications are noted.

responseSet.title: Father—Daughter Incest: Unconscious Fantasy and Social Fact
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Father—Daughter Incest

Unconscious Fantasy and Social Fact

Diana Diamond PHD

Diana Diamond

Diana Diamond  

This article examines the etiological role of incest as unconscious fantasy and incest as social fact in producing psychopathology. It reevaluates the importance of the Freudian theory of fantasy for an understanding of father-daughter incest, with particular attention to the relationship between fantasy and memory and between conscious and unconscious fantasy. Although the importance of acknowledging the social fact of incest is affirmed, I maintain that an analysis of the mental representation of the incest experience in fantasy is as crucial to the treatment of incest victims as is an acknowledgment of the primary etiological role of real victimization in producing pathological states. Case material is presented to illustrate the ways in which the reality of incest may combine with stage-specific fantasies to determine the nature and extent of the incest victim' pathology.

responseSet.title: Symbols of Terror: The Witch/Vampire, the Spider, and the Shark
responseSet.authorMast: Robert C. Lane and  Saralea E. Chazan
responseSet.authorCitation: Lane, R. C. & Chazan, S. E.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symbols of Terror

The Witch/Vampire, the Spider, and the Shark

Robert Lane PHD and Saralea Chazan PHD

Saralea Chazan

Saralea Chazan  

Three symbols of terror are examined as representations of preoedipal conflicts that intrude upon conscious awareness and interfere with current functioning. To understand each of these symbols and their origins in a state of chronic terror, it is necessary to understand the complete life history of the patient. All three symbols—the witch/vampire, the spider, and the shark—relate to earliest experiences within the parent/infant dyad. We suggest that although all three symbols relate to the dimension of oral aggression, they can be placed conceptually along a continuum of distance from the quality of humanness. Clinical examples are given to illustrate the use of these symbols by patients in treatment and their theoretical implications.
